questions.question: A light meter held 12 in. from a small portable lamp reads 200 fc. An obstruction forces the lamp back to 24 in. from the examination surface. The illuminance at the surface will now be approximately:
options: 100 fc, still meeting the Article 9 minimum
About 141 fc
About 50 fc
About 25 fc
questions.explanation: Light from a point source obeys the inverse square law: doubling the distance quarters the illuminance, so 200 fc falls to about 50 fc — now below the 100 fc (1076 lx) minimum ASME Section V, Article 9 requires at the examination surface. The lighting must be restored and re-verified with the calibrated meter before examining. Answering 100 fc assumes a simple inverse (linear) falloff, which is not how a point source behaves.

questions.question: Under NAS410, near-vision acuity for NDT personnel is demonstrated by reading:
options: Jaeger 1 (or equivalent) at not less than 30.5 cm (12 in.), one eye, corrected or uncorrected, verified annually
Jaeger 2 at 50 cm with both eyes simultaneously, verified every five years
Snellen 20/20 at 20 ft (6 m) without corrective lenses
An Ishihara plate series at reading distance, verified every six months
questions.explanation: NAS410 requires reading Jaeger 1 or equivalent at not less than 30.5 cm (12 in.) with at least one eye, corrected or uncorrected, annually; written practices based on SNT-TC-1A commonly specify Jaeger 2 at the same distance and interval. Color perception — distinguishing the colors or shades of gray used in the method — is the separate check, verified per SNT-TC-1A at intervals not exceeding five years. Distance (Snellen) acuity is not the requirement: VT lives at near-vision working distances.

questions.question: The feature that distinguishes a videoscope from a fiberscope or a rigid borescope is:
options: A coherent fiber bundle whose fibers keep the same relative positions at both ends
A CCD or CMOS imager at the distal tip that converts the image to an electronic signal at the point of capture, so working length no longer degrades the picture
A rod-lens relay train that carries the optical image up a rigid tube to the eyepiece
An incoherent fiber bundle that carries both the image and the illumination
questions.explanation: The videoscope digitizes the image at the distal tip, so the picture survives any working length — and its stereo or structured-light measuring tips size cracks, nicks, and missing material in place against engine-manual limits. The coherent bundle (with its honeycomb pixelation) defines the fiberscope, the rod-lens relay defines the rigid borescope, and an incoherent bundle can only deliver illumination, never an image.

questions.question: Internal laminate conditions in a thin fiberglass component must be evaluated visually. The appropriate technique under ASME Section V, Article 9 is:
options: Direct VT with a 10x magnifier and low-angle raking light
Remote VT with a 90-degree side-viewing rigid borescope
Direct VT after coating the surface matte white to suppress glare
Translucent VT — a strong directional illuminator diffusing light through the thickness so internal conditions show as shadows and bright zones in the transmitted glow
questions.explanation: Translucent examination is Article 9's third technique, a supplement to direct VT for translucent materials such as fiberglass laminates: light transmitted through the thickness reveals internal laminate conditions. Magnifiers and raking light only interrogate the surface, a borescope needs an internal cavity to enter, and a coating would block the very light path the technique depends on.

questions.question: An opened centrifugal pump shows spongy, deeply pitted metal concentrated near the impeller vane inlets and low-pressure faces. The damage mechanism is:
options: Wire drawing from high-velocity leakage flow
Hydrogen blistering
Cavitation — collapsing vapor bubbles hammering the surface
Galling from assembly contact
questions.explanation: Cavitation is the classic pump find: vapor bubbles form in the low-pressure region and collapse against the impeller, hammering the metal into a spongy, deeply pitted texture concentrated at the vane inlets. Wire drawing cuts smooth, wormhole-like channels across valve seating surfaces, hydrogen blistering raises bulges on vessel plate, and galling is adhesive damage on mating stems and threads — texture plus location reads the surface back to its mechanism.

questions.question: Visual examination of threaded fasteners concentrates on the head-to-shank fillet and the first engaged thread because:
options: These are the points of highest stress concentration, where fatigue cracks initiate
These are the only locations where corrosion can attack a fastener
Thread pitch can only be measured at these two points
Protective plating is thickest there and hides discontinuities
questions.explanation: A threaded fastener concentrates stress at exactly two places — the head-to-shank fillet and the first engaged thread — so that is where fatigue cracks live and where the eye (often aided by a low-power magnifier) goes first. Corrosion can attack anywhere on the fastener, and pitch is checked along the thread with a pitch gauge to detect stretch: a stretched or necked bolt has yielded and is replaced, never reworked.

questions.question: A forging is suspected of containing bursts from improper hot working. Visual testing is not the appropriate method because:
options: Bursts occur only in castings, never in forgings
Bursts are internal ruptures, and VT sees only surfaces the optical path reaches — finding them is volumetric territory, ultrasonic testing's job
Forged surfaces are too reflective for any visual technique
ASME Section V prohibits visual examination of hot-worked products
questions.explanation: Forging bursts are internal ruptures from improper hot working, and no visual examination will find them — VT's fundamental limit is that it detects only what the optical path can reach. The forging's visual list is its surface signatures: laps folded over near the flash or parting line, seams carried forward from billet defects, scale pits, and underfill. Internal soundness requires a volumetric method such as UT.

questions.question: By convention, a visual indication is classified as linear when:
options: It is longer than 1/16 in. (1.6 mm) in any direction
It lies parallel to the weld axis
It is visible without magnification
Its length exceeds three times its width
questions.explanation: The conventional rule classifies an indication as linear when its length exceeds three times its width, and the classification matters because linear indications almost always face tighter acceptance limits than rounded ones. One limit is universal regardless of classification: cracks are prohibited — no fabrication code grants a crack an allowable size, in part because a crack's visible length says little about its true extent.

questions.question: Examining a polished part through an inspection mirror, an examiner sees a fine bright line. After the mirror is cleaned and repositioned, the line is gone and the surface itself shows no interruption. The original indication is properly classified as:
options: False — an artifact of the viewing conditions, not caused by a discontinuity
Nonrelevant — a real feature of the part arising from design or processing
Relevant — every indication must proceed to evaluation against acceptance criteria
A defect, because it was observed during a formal examination
questions.explanation: Interpretation sorts every indication into one of three bins: false (artifacts of the viewing conditions — shadow lines, glare streaks, smeared compound, a scratch in an inspection mirror), nonrelevant (real part features from design or processing, such as a machining step), or relevant (caused by a discontinuity). Only relevant indications proceed to evaluation, and 'defect' is reserved for a discontinuity that evaluation shows to exceed the written acceptance criteria.

questions.question: An inspector measures 0.5 mm (0.020 in.) of undercut on two different steel weldments governed by AWS D1.1. The undercut is acceptable on the first member but rejectable on the second. The most likely reason is:
options: The second weld was viewed under a magnifier, which tightens the tabulated limit
The second member is cyclically loaded with stress transverse to the weld, where the undercut limit tightens to as little as 0.01 in. (0.25 mm)
Undercut is prohibited in any amount on all D1.1 weldments, so the first call was an error
The acceptance limit depends on the qualification level of the welder who made the joint
questions.explanation: AWS D1.1 tabulates visual criteria by service: undercut on the order of 1/32 in. (1 mm) for most statically loaded members, tightening to as little as 0.01 in. (0.25 mm) on cyclically loaded members stressed transverse to the weld. A 0.020 in. reading passes one table and fails the other — the same discontinuity can be a defect under one set of criteria and acceptable under another, which is why the governing document is identified before evaluation. Viewing aids and welder qualifications never change a written limit.
